gamma_events.title: Highest temperature in Munich on June 22?
gamma_events.description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the Munich Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 22 Jun '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Munich Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/de/munich/EDDM.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent European model runs, including ECMWF ensembles, indicate Munich will experience peak temperatures of 31–33°C on June 22 under building high pressure and southerly flow ahead of a weak front, supporting the tight clustering of market-implied odds around those thresholds. Ensemble spreads and local factors such as afternoon convective cloud development or timing of any showers introduce realistic uncertainty that could cap readings near 31°C or allow brief clearing to push toward 34°C. With the summer solstice approaching and recent Central European warmth providing a warm baseline, forecasters emphasize that small shifts in boundary-layer moisture or insolation will determine the exact daily maximum recorded at official stations. Updated short-range guidance expected within 24–48 hours will likely narrow the range further.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current numerical weather prediction models, including recent ECMWF and GFS runs, indicate a warm air mass advecting over southern Germany, supporting a June 23 maximum near 32 °C in Munich with light rain or showers possible midday. This consensus underpins the market’s tight clustering around 31–33 °C, where small differences in cloud timing, boundary-layer mixing, and precipitation onset create the observed spread between leading outcomes. Ensemble variability remains modest two days out, yet traders weigh the risk that earlier or heavier showers could cap the peak at 31 °C versus clearer intervals allowing 33 °C, with official DWD observations on the day serving as the resolution source.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from sources like AccuWeather and numerical models point to highs near 79–84°F for New York City on June 21, 2026, aligning with the market’s tight clustering around 82–85°F as the most probable range. This positioning reflects current atmospheric conditions dominated by a stable high-pressure pattern and near-maximum solar insolation at the summer solstice, supporting modest daytime heating without strong warm-air advection. eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from sources like AccuWeather and numerical models point to highs near 79–84°F for New York City on June 21, 2026, aligning with the market’s tight clustering around 82–85°F as the most probable range. Traders are weighting official guidance showing conditions close to or slightly above the June normal of 81°F, with limited upside from climatological analogs.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the London City Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/gb/london/EGLC.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from sources like AccuWeather and numerical models point to highs near 79–84°F for New York City on June 21, 2026, aligning with the market’s tight clustering around 82–85°F as the most probable range. This positioning reflects current atmospheric conditions dominated by a stable high-pressure pattern and near-maximum solar insolation at the summer solstice, supporting modest daytime heating without strong warm-air advection. Subtle model differences in boundary-layer mixing, dew point profiles, and any residual cloud cover explain the near-even split between the 82–83°F and 84–85°F outcomes, while lower probabilities for 88°F+ reflect the low likelihood of significant amplification from regional flow. Traders are weighting official guidance showing conditions close to or slightly above the June normal of 81°F, with limited upside from climatological analogs.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts for Tokyo around June 22, 2026, show expected highs clustering in the mid-20s Celsius amid the ongoing Baiu rainy season, which features persistent cloud cover and frequent precipitation from the stationary front. This reduces solar insolation and prevents stronger daytime heating typical of clearer summer conditions, supporting the market's close probabilities on 25°C and 26°C as the most likely outcomes. Sea breezes from Tokyo Bay and elevated humidity further moderate peaks, while any brief breaks in cloudiness could allow brief rises toward 27–28°C. Official models from agencies like the Japan Meteorological Agency emphasize this transitional pattern, with limited upside risk above 29°C given current steering patterns and moisture.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent European model runs, including ECMWF ensembles, indicate Munich will experience peak temperatures of 31–33°C on June 22 under building high pressure and southerly flow ahead of a weak front, supporting the tight clustering of market-implied odds around those thresholds. Ensemble spreads and local factors such as afternoon convective cloud development or timing of any showers introduce realistic uncertainty that could cap readings near 31°C or allow brief clearing to push toward 34°C. With the summer solstice approaching and recent Central European warmth providing a warm baseline, forecasters emphasize that small shifts in boundary-layer moisture or insolation will determine the exact daily maximum recorded at official stations. Updated short-range guidance expected within 24–48 hours will likely narrow the range further.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current ensemble forecasts from the Met Office and European models indicate peak temperatures of 28–30°C as the most likely outcome for London on June 22, driven by high pressure building over southern England combined with southerly airflow advecting warmer air masses. These values sit near the June climatological average high of 28°C yet reflect modest positive anomalies in recent model runs, with limited cloud cover and light winds favoring daytime heating. Uncertainty around the exact maximum arises from potential afternoon convection or subtle steering variations, keeping probabilities distributed across 28–30°C while lower odds attach to extremes outside this range. Updated model guidance over the next 48 hours will further constrain resolution criteria tied to official observations.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from sources like AccuWeather and numerical models point to highs near 79–84°F for New York City on June 21, 2026, aligning with the market’s tight clustering around 82–85°F as the most probable range. This positioning reflects current atmospheric conditions dominated by a stable high-pressure pattern and near-maximum solar insolation at the summer solstice, supporting modest daytime heating without strong warm-air advection. Subtle model differences in boundary-layer mixing, dew point profiles, and any residual cloud cover explain the near-even split between the 82–83°F and 84–85°F outcomes, while lower probabilities for 88°F+ reflect the low likelihood of significant amplification from regional flow. Traders are weighting official guidance showing conditions close to or slightly above the June normal of 81°F, with limited upside from climatological analogs.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent National Weather Service and model guidance indicate partly to mostly cloudy skies with periods of rain or showers possible across the NYC metro on June 22, limiting daytime heating and keeping highs likely in the mid-70s. This uncertainty in precipitation timing, cloud thickness, and any breaks of sun has produced closely bunched market probabilities centered on 74–77 °F. Key differentiating factors include the strength of onshore flow, boundary-layer moisture, and exact convective coverage, all of which can shift maximum temperatures by several degrees; official resolution relies on the highest reading at LaGuardia or Central Park. Updated model runs and NWS briefings over the next 24 hours will provide the clearest signals for traders.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from sources like AccuWeather and numerical models point to highs near 79–84°F for New York City on June 21, 2026, aligning with the market’s tight clustering around 82–85°F as the most probable range. This positioning reflects current atmospheric conditions dominated by a stable high-pressure pattern and near-maximum solar insolation at the summer solstice, supporting modest daytime heating without strong warm-air advection. Subtle model differences in boundary-layer mixing, dew point profiles, and any residual cloud cover explain the near-even split between the 82–83°F and 84–85°F outcomes, while lower probabilities for 88°F+ reflect the low likelihood of significant amplification from regional flow. Traders are weighting official guidance showing conditions close to or slightly above the June normal of 81°F, with limited upside from climatological analogs.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent European model runs, including ECMWF ensembles, indicate Munich will experience peak temperatures of 31–33°C on June 22 under building high pressure and southerly flow ahead of a weak front, supporting the tight clustering of market-implied odds around those thresholds. Ensemble spreads and local factors such as afternoon convective cloud development or timing of any showers introduce realistic uncertainty that could cap readings near 31°C or allow brief clearing to push toward 34°C. With the summer solstice approaching and recent Central European warmth providing a warm baseline, forecasters emphasize that small shifts in boundary-layer moisture or insolation will determine the exact daily maximum recorded at official stations. Updated short-range guidance expected within 24–48 hours will likely narrow the range further.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from sources like AccuWeather and numerical models point to highs near 79–84°F for New York City on June 21, 2026, aligning with the market’s tight clustering around 82–85°F as the most probable range. This positioning reflects current atmospheric conditions dominated by a stable high-pressure pattern and near-maximum solar insolation at the summer solstice, supporting modest daytime heating without strong warm-air advection. Subtle model differences in boundary-layer mixing, dew point profiles, and any residual cloud cover explain the near-even split between the 82–83°F and 84–85°F outcomes, while lower probabilities for 88°F+ reflect the low likelihood of significant amplification from regional flow. Traders are weighting official guidance showing conditions close to or slightly above the June normal of 81°F, with limited upside from climatological analogs.

This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the Munich Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 22 Jun '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Munich Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/de/munich/EDDM.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent European model runs, including ECMWF ensembles, indicate Munich will experience peak temperatures of 31–33°C on June 22 under building high pressure and southerly flow ahead of a weak front, supporting the tight clustering of market-implied odds around those thresholds. Ensemble spreads and local factors such as afternoon convective cloud development or timing of any showers introduce realistic uncertainty that could cap readings near 31°C or allow brief clearing to push toward 34°C. With the summer solstice approaching and recent Central European warmth providing a warm baseline, forecasters emphasize that small shifts in boundary-layer moisture or insolation will determine the exact daily maximum recorded at official stations. Updated short-range guidance expected within 24–48 hours will likely narrow the range further.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current numerical weather prediction models, including recent ECMWF and GFS runs, indicate a warm air mass advecting over southern Germany, supporting a June 23 maximum near 32 °C in Munich with light rain or showers possible midday. This consensus underpins the market’s tight clustering around 31–33 °C, where small differences in cloud timing, boundary-layer mixing, and precipitation onset create the observed spread between leading outcomes. Ensemble variability remains modest two days out, yet traders weigh the risk that earlier or heavier showers could cap the peak at 31 °C versus clearer intervals allowing 33 °C, with official DWD observations on the day serving as the resolution source.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current ensemble forecasts from the Met Office and European models indicate peak temperatures of 28–30°C as the most likely outcome for London on June 22, driven by high pressure building over southern England combined with southerly airflow advecting warmer air masses. These values sit near the June climatological average high of 28°C yet reflect modest positive anomalies in recent model runs, with limited cloud cover and light winds favoring daytime heating. Uncertainty around the exact maximum arises from potential afternoon convection or subtle steering variations, keeping probabilities distributed across 28–30°C while lower odds attach to extremes outside this range. Updated model guidance over the next 48 hours will further constrain resolution criteria tied to official observations.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Met Office forecasts highlight a strengthening high-pressure system and warm southerly flow driving an early-summer heatwave across southern England, with central London temperatures potentially peaking near 35–37 °C on June 24. Ensemble model runs show modest spread around these values due to uncertain cloud cover and boundary-layer mixing, while climatological baselines place average June maxima near 20–22 °C. The amber extreme-heat warning through mid-week and the proximity to the UK June record of 35.6 °C anchor trader focus on the 36–38 °C band, though any overnight cooling or Atlantic front arrival could cap readings at 35 °C or below. Updated guidance expected within 24 hours will further refine land-breeze and urban-heat-island effects.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from sources like AccuWeather and numerical models point to highs near 79–84°F for New York City on June 21, 2026, aligning with the market’s tight clustering around 82–85°F as the most probable range. This positioning reflects current atmospheric conditions dominated by a stable high-pressure pattern and near-maximum solar insolation at the summer solstice, supporting modest daytime heating without strong warm-air advection. Subtle model differences in boundary-layer mixing, dew point profiles, and any residual cloud cover explain the near-even split between the 82–83°F and 84–85°F outcomes, while lower probabilities for 88°F+ reflect the low likelihood of significant amplification from regional flow. Traders are weighting official guidance showing conditions close to or slightly above the June normal of 81°F, with limited upside from climatological analogs.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current ensemble forecasts from the Met Office and European models indicate peak temperatures of 28–30°C as the most likely outcome for London on June 22, driven by high pressure building over southern England combined with southerly airflow advecting warmer air masses. These values sit near the June climatological average high of 28°C yet reflect modest positive anomalies in recent model runs, with limited cloud cover and light winds favoring daytime heating. Uncertainty around the exact maximum arises from potential afternoon convection or subtle steering variations, keeping probabilities distributed across 28–30°C while lower odds attach to extremes outside this range. Updated model guidance over the next 48 hours will further constrain resolution criteria tied to official observations.

eventMetadata.context_description: A strengthening high-pressure system and associated heat dome are driving the current market positioning, with official Met Office guidance and ensemble model runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts pointing to peak temperatures of 34–38 °C across London on 23 June. Warm advection from the near-continent, combined with clear skies and light winds, favors rapid daytime warming, while recent amber heat-health alerts reflect growing model consensus that 35 °C will be exceeded. Forecaster updates in the past 48 hours have nudged higher-end scenarios toward 37–38 °C in parts of the southeast, yet spread among runs leaves room for a lower outcome if cloud or a slightly stronger sea breeze develops. Final guidance from the Met Office and updated high-resolution runs on 22 June will be the key catalysts likely to shift trader probabilities before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Met Office forecasts highlight a strengthening high-pressure system and warm southerly flow driving an early-summer heatwave across southern England, with central London temperatures potentially peaking near 35–37 °C on June 24. Ensemble model runs show modest spread around these values due to uncertain cloud cover and boundary-layer mixing, while climatological baselines place average June maxima near 20–22 °C. The amber extreme-heat warning through mid-week and the proximity to the UK June record of 35.6 °C anchor trader focus on the 36–38 °C band, though any overnight cooling or Atlantic front arrival could cap readings at 35 °C or below. Updated guidance expected within 24 hours will further refine land-breeze and urban-heat-island effects.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Tokyo Haneda Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/jp/tokyo/RJTT.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecast model runs from the Japan Meteorological Agency show modest disagreement on Tokyo's June 24 daytime high, producing the tight clustering of market-implied probabilities around 24–26 °C. Southerly flow ahead of an approaching trough, combined with variable cloud cover and urban heat-island effects, creates the primary uncertainty range. Ensemble guidance places the most likely maximum near the seasonal normal of roughly 25 °C, yet small shifts in timing of any coastal sea breeze or afternoon convection could easily move the observed high one degree either way. Traders are therefore weighting the latest deterministic and ensemble outputs that will be refreshed within the next 24–48 hours.
